questions.questionText: Cryptococcus neoformans meningitis is diagnosed by:
questions.optionB: India ink preparation (encapsulated yeast in CSF) and cryptococcal antigen (CrAg) detection — most common fungal meningitis in HIV
questions.explanation: Cryptococcus neoformans: encapsulated yeast, urease positive. Found in pigeon droppings. Most common cause of fungal meningitis (especially HIV/AIDS, CD4<100). Diagnosis: (1) India ink preparation: encapsulated yeast (clear halo) in CSF (sensitivity ~50-75%), (2) Cryptococcal antigen (CrAg) latex agglutination/lateral flow assay: most sensitive and specific (>95%). CSF: elevated pressure (key finding — can cause blindness), lymphocytic pleocytosis, low glucose. Treatment: amphotericin B + flucytosine (induction) → fluconazole (consolidation/maintenance). Reference: Ananthanarayan — Cryptococcus.

questions.questionText: Plasmodium falciparum malaria is the most dangerous species because it:
questions.optionB: Infects RBCs of ALL ages (high parasitemia), causes cerebral malaria, and shows multiple ring forms and banana-shaped gametocytes
questions.explanation: P. falciparum: malignant tertian malaria (48h cycle, but often irregular fever). Most dangerous: infects ALL age RBCs → high parasitemia. Causes: cerebral malaria (RBC sequestration via PfEMP1 adhesion to brain endothelium), severe anemia, renal failure, ARDS, algid malaria (shock), blackwater fever (hemoglobinuria). Smear: multiple ring forms ('headphone' appearance), banana/crescent-shaped gametocytes. NO hypnozoite stage (unlike P. vivax/ovale). Drug resistance common (chloroquine-resistant). Treatment: artemisinin combination therapy (ACT). Reference: Ananthanarayan — P. falciparum.

questions.optionB: Amoebic dysentery (flask-shaped ulcers in colon) and amoebic liver abscess (anchovy sauce pus)
questions.explanation: E. histolytica: intestinal protozoan. Infective form: cyst (4 nuclei, ingested in contaminated food/water). Pathogenic form: trophozoite (single nucleus, ingested RBCs — erythrophagocytosis). Intestinal: amoebic dysentery — bloody mucoid diarrhea, flask-shaped undermined ulcers in cecum and ascending colon. Extraintestinal: amoebic liver abscess (right lobe, single, 'anchovy sauce/chocolate' colored sterile pus — no odor), may rupture into peritoneum/pleura. Diagnosis: stool microscopy (cyst/trophozoite), serology (ELISA for anti-amoebic Ab). Treatment: metronidazole + diloxanide furoate. Reference: Ananthanarayan — E. histolytica.

questions.questionText: Aspergillus fumigatus causes which type of invasive infection in immunocompromised patients?
questions.optionB: Invasive pulmonary aspergillosis (IPA — angioinvasion, hemorrhagic infarcts), and allergic bronchopulmonary aspergillosis (ABPA) in asthmatics
questions.explanation: Aspergillus fumigatus: septate hyphae branching at 45° angles (acute angles) — 'V-shaped' dichotomous branching. Invasive aspergillosis (IA): immunocompromised (neutropenic, transplant) → angioinvasion → hemorrhagic pulmonary infarcts, brain abscess. ABPA: hypersensitivity to Aspergillus in asthmatics (IgE elevated, eosinophilia, central bronchiectasis). Aspergilloma: 'fungus ball' in pre-existing lung cavity (hemoptysis). Diagnosis: galactomannan antigen, β-D-glucan, CT (halo sign — IPA). Treatment: voriconazole (IPA first-line). Reference: Ananthanarayan — Aspergillus.

questions.questionText: Wuchereria bancrofti is the causative agent of:
questions.optionB: Lymphatic filariasis (elephantiasis — transmitted by Culex mosquito, microfilariae show nocturnal periodicity)
questions.explanation: W. bancrofti: filarial nematode causing lymphatic filariasis. Vector: Culex mosquito (most common), also Anopheles, Aedes. Adult worms live in lymphatic vessels → lymphatic obstruction → lymphedema → elephantiasis (chronic — massive swelling of limbs, genitals). Microfilariae: nocturnal periodicity (peak in peripheral blood 10 PM-2 AM — adapted to vector biting time). Diagnosis: nocturnal blood smear (microfilariae), ICT (immunochromatographic test for circulating filarial antigen — can be done anytime). Treatment: DEC (diethylcarbamazine). MDA: DEC + albendazole. Reference: Ananthanarayan — W. bancrofti.
